data.title: U.S. stocks sink as war against Iran intensifies, oil prices keep climbing
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were lower on Thursday as oil prices continued to climb due to the escalating U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.2%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.5%, and the Dow Jones (DJI) was -1.3%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points higher at 4.25%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 9 basis points to 3.75%.

Crude oil (CL1:COM) rose 8.3% to $94 per barrel on Thursday, while Brent (CO1:COM) was also higher at $99.

“I find it hard not to have a central scenario where oil prices will remain very high for a long time, higher than the market current prices,” said Olivier Blanchard, Robert Solow Professor of economics emeritus at MIT.

“There is no reason, whether or not Trump declares that war is over, to think that Iran will not continue for some time to threaten to destroy the ships that try. Why should they stop? The risk will thus remain sufficiently high that most non-Iranian ships will not take the risk. Thus, the shortfall of 20 million barrels a day is likely to last for long,” he added.

The conflict in the Middle East has triggered one of the largest supply shocks of global oil markets in history, leading to disruption of almost 7.5% of the worldwide supply, according to the International Energy Agency.

In economic news, the initial jobless claims barely budged in the past week. The U.S. trade deficit narrowed more than expected in January.

Also, housing starts unexpectedly rose in January, while building permits dropped more than consensus.

The Federal Reserve balance sheet will be released later in the day.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum jumps after winning two analyst upgrades
data.description:
Occidental Petroleum (OXY) +5.9% in Thursday's trading, rallying to its best level since August 2024 after Wells Fargo upgraded shares to Overweight from Underweight with a $69 price target, up from $47, while Piper Sandler raised the stock to Overweight from Neutral with a $66 PT, up from $54.

Wells Fargo's Sam Margolin double upgraded Occidental (OXY), saying the stock's peer-leading oil sensitivity is "both an opportunity and a risk," but stronger Permian Basin productivity and improved capital intensity now support a more constructive setup.

Margolin said his prior Sell-equivalent rating was driven by return of capital constraints due to preferred equity, which - barring a $4/share return of capital trigger - are unlikely to be redeemed until 2029, and while the constraint remains unless oil stays above $100/bbl, "the step change in capital efficiency reflected in 4Q25 earnings supports regular dividend growth and buyback opportunities in the intervening years."

Oxy (OXY) has adjusted its Permian spending plan from $3.9B to $3.1B while maintaining
production growth, which is enabled by a combination of factors including strong underlying productivity and enhanced oil recovery which dampens observable base decline, and the analyst said he believes the company can produce slightly ahead of guidance in 2026 and resume an accelerated growth trend in 2027 back to $3.5B in capital.

Piper Sandler's Mark Lear views Occidental (OXY) as one of the best operators in the Delaware Basin that continues to deliver among the best operating performance in the basin despite some
degradation in FY 2025, and noted the company's FY 2026 guidance was much more capital efficient than initial soft guidance, with ~$800M less capital to drive a similar level of production.

Despite strong YTD performance, Occidental (OXY) trades roughly in line with large-cap peers on estimated FY 2026 strip EBITDA and FCF/EV yield and demonstrates some of the strongest leverage to oil upside, Lear wrote.

data.title: Esquire Financial to acquire Signature Bancorporation in all-stock deal
data.description:
Esquire Financial Holdings (ESQ) is set to acquire Signature Bancorporation in an all-stock transaction.

ESQ shares were 6.69% higher to $105.67 during Thursday afternoon.

Signature shareholders will receive a fixed exchange ratio of 2.63 Esquire shares for each stock they own.

The company statement said the exchange ratio is subject to an adjustment based on the disposition value of certain Signature Bank loans. Signature has initiated a sale process for Schedule A Loans, with the disposition expected prior to the closing of the deal.

The per-share value equates to $260.48 for Signature shareholders based on the March 11 closing price of Esquire, bringing the aggregate transaction value to ~$348.4M.

The combined entity is expected to have ~$4.8B in assets at closing.

The acquisition is estimated to help Esquire expand into the Chicago market.

The transaction is estimated to be 23% accretive to the combined company's 2027 GAAP EPS, while no associated revenue enhancement is projected.

The transaction is ~11% accretive to Esquire's tangible book value, and assumes 5% cost savings.

The resulting company's board will consist of eleven directors, including nine directors from Esquire and two directors from Signature. Signature Board Chairman Leonard Caronia and CEO Michael O'Rourke are set to join the board of the merged entity.

The transaction is set to close in the third quarter.

data.title: trivago adds three new positions to C-suite to focus on AI, strategy
data.description:
• Trivago (TRVG) has added three new positions to its C-suite as the company sharpens its focus on artificial intelligence and commercial strategy.
  • “Capturing this opportunity fully requires dedicated senior ownership. Dedicated domain leaders in technology, data intelligence, and commercial strategy mean clearer accountability, faster decisions, and senior attention precisely where execution is happening,” the company said in a statement.
  • In his new position as Chief Intelligence Officer, Alexander Volkmann’s focus will be on trivago’s (TRVG) data strategy, analytics, and AI/ML capabilities with direct accountability for the Hotel Search data science and analytics team.
  • Sherin Hegazy has been promoted to Chief Commercial Officer from her current position as Global Head of Partnerships, and Ioannis Papdopoulos to Chief Technology Officer.
  • All three individuals have been appointed from within the company.
  • The announcement has done little to stem the pressure on the trivago (TRVG) shares, down for a fifth straight day and adding to its year-over-year loss of 30%.

data.title: The stock market is not reacting enough to the Middle East conflict – BCA Research’s Marko Papic
data.description:
Marko Papic, macro and geopolitical strategist at BCA Research, warned that markets are underestimating the severity and duration of the ongoing Iran tensions.

“The market is not reacting enough because there is a very large percent probability that it does last quite long,” the strategist said in an interview with CNBC.

Even oil markets, despite recent spot price (CO1:COM), (CL1:COM) increases, are displaying dangerous complacency.

Papic believes oil prices need to climb significantly higher before the crisis can reach a resolution. He projected that prices must reach $120 to $130 per barrel, expressing concern about “complacency in both the equity markets risk, generally speaking, but also oil.”

The strategist outlined the potential for a severe U.S. military response, noting that President Trump has signaled intentions for a punitive air campaign against Iran. Anyone who believes Iran can maintain closure of the Strait of Hormuz for an extended period is making a significant assumption, Papic argued.

Such a stance would mean “this regime is willing [to] effectively turning the country into a parking lot over those six months.”

Despite Iran’s tough public rhetoric, Papic pointed to signs that Tehran may be seeking ways to de-escalate behind the scenes. He noted that Iran is “talking behind the scenes to a slew of countries” and has equivocated on the total closure of the straits. Reports also suggest some Indian-flagged tankers have been permitted passage through the contested waterway.

Drawing historical parallels, Papic reminded investors that similar situations occurred during the 1980s tanker wars between Iraq and Iran. During that conflict, the U.S. Navy, joined by other nations including the Soviet Union, successfully cleared mines and established naval escort missions.

The resolution of the current crisis comes down to what Papic described as a “mathematical equation” weighing Iranian pain tolerance against the world’s ability to clear the straits and utilize global reserves.

Unlike the 1970s and 1980s, today’s world has more plentiful reserves to buy time. Ultimately, Iran would have to endure “extreme pain from a punitive air campaign” longer than those reserves can sustain global markets.

data.title: xAI's co-founder exits continue, Zihang Dai departs, another may leave soon: report
data.description:
The exodus of co-founders from Elon Musk's AI startup xAI (X.AI) ‌has continued, with the latest being Zihang Dai, Business Insider reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter. 

Dai left xAI earlier this week. His xAI badge has vanished from his X profile. Dai was a member of the technical staff at the company, the report added.

Another co-founder of xAI, Guodong Zhang, has informed people he plans to leave in the coming days, the report noted. His LinkedIn profile shows he is still part of xAI.

xAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Dai's departure comes on the heels of the exits of other co-founders of the Grok AI chatbot maker, including Toby Pohlen, Jimmy Ba, Tony Wu, and Greg Yang.

Last month Musk's space company SpaceX (SPACE) announced it was acquiring xAI (X.AI).

After Dai and Zhang exit, only two of the 11 people who started the company with Musk in 2023 — Manuel Kroiss and Ross Nordeen — will remain, the report noted.

Zhang, who previously worked at Google DeepMind, reports directly to Musk and led two of xAI's vital projects: Grok Code and Grok Imagine. He was given a larger role at xAI earlier this year, shortly before Wu left the company, the report added.

On Wednesday, Musk told the Abundance Conference that "Grok is currently behind in coding."

"The reason I was late for this was that I was just in a giant sort of all-hands on coding, going through all the things that need to happen to essentially exceed our competitors on coding, which I think we'll do," said Musk, the report noted.

data.title: The biggest shareholder of Wynn Resorts wants to take over Caesars Entertainment
data.description:
Tilman Fertitta continues to make moves in the Las Vegas casino scene. If he is successful with his acquisition bid for Caesars Entertainment (CZR), he would gain control of eight Las Vegas-area properties, including Caesars Palace, Harrah's, and Paris on the Strip.

Fertitta is also currently the largest single shareholder of Wynn Resorts (WYNN), with an economic interest of 12.5% in the company following his 2025 share purchases and accounting for options. He holds the position through Fertitta Entertainment and related entities. The Elaine Wynn Family Trust is believed to be the next largest shareholder, with a position between 8% and 9% in the casino company. Bank of America analyst Shaun Kelly noted that it is possible that Fertitta's WYNN stake could be used to help finance the Caesars (CZR) takeover.

Looking back, Fertitta entered casinos in 2005 by buying the Golden Nugget Las Vegas and Laughlin properties through Landry’s, marking his move from restaurants into gaming. He later expanded the Golden Nugget brand to Atlantic City, Biloxi, and Lake Charles, including purchasing the former Trump Marina in 2011. Today, his Fertitta Entertainment controls multiple Golden Nugget casinos and related online gaming operations. He also holds over 6 acres of Las Vegas Strip property at the southeast corner of Las Vegas Boulevard and Harmon Avenue, close to MGM Resorts' (MGM) MGM Grand and Aria properties and Caesars' (CZR) Planet Hollywood.

Shares of Caesars Entertainment (VZR) fell 1.8% in Thursday afternoon trading after jumping more than 11% on Wednesday.

data.title: Jones Act explained: Trump weighs wartime suspension to curb rising oil prices
data.description:
With the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran now in its second week, Strait of Hormuz traffic halted and crude oil topping $90 per barrel, U.S. President Donald Trump is weighing suspending the 1920 Jones Act to unlock domestic fuel flows and blunt shortages.

Trump's team is eyeing 30-day waivers to let foreign tankers shuttle Gulf Coast fuel to East Coast refiners, invoking national defense exceptions last used after Hurricane Fiona in 2022.

What is the Jones Act?

In the aftermath of World War I, the United States found itself reexamining the strength of its shipping industry. The war had revealed vulnerabilities in America’s maritime capabilities, and lawmakers understood the need for a robust domestic fleet, both for commerce and national defense. Out of this moment of reflection and urgency emerged the Merchant Marine Act of 1920, commonly known as the Jones Act.

At its core, the Jones Act regulates what is known as “cabotage” or the transport of goods between U.S. ports. Under Section 27 of the Merchant Marine Act, vessels engaged in domestic trade must satisfy four requirements: they must be built in U.S. shipyards, owned by companies with at least 75% American ownership, registered under the U.S. flag, and crewed primarily by American citizens.

These strict provisions were designed to ensure that domestic shipping remained an American enterprise, firmly anchored in national sovereignty.

The law allows waivers in times of crisis, typically when national defense or emergencies make adherence impractical. During World War II, the U.S. government temporarily set aside many Jones Act restrictions to prioritize military needs. More recently, the Biden administration granted a waiver after Hurricane Fiona struck Puerto Rico in 2022, permitting a foreign-flagged vessel to deliver fuel amid an acute energy shortage.

Despite these exceptions, the Act continues to generate controversy. After the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the U.S. banned Russian oil imports, a policy that exposed logistical challenges for states like Hawaii, which had long relied on Russian crude.

Because the Jones Act restricts the use of foreign ships for domestic transport, moving U.S.-produced oil to Hawaii became both difficult and costly, forcing the state to continue importing foreign alternatives.

Penalties for violating the Act can be severe. In 2017, Furie Operating Alaska LLC faced a $10 million fine for using a foreign-flagged heavy lift vessel to transport a drilling rig from the Gulf of Mexico to Alaska without the required waiver.

More than a century later, the Jones Act remains one of the most debated maritime laws in U.S. history. Proponents argue that its repeal would jeopardize national security and American maritime jobs, while critics maintain that reform could lower consumer costs and ease financial burdens on island economies.

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC)

data.title: Unit Corporation GAAP EPS of $5.15, revenue of $24.72M
data.description:
• Unit Corporation press release (UNTC): Q4 GAAP EPS of $5.15.
  • Revenue of $24.72M.

  • During the year ended December 31, 2025, the Company repurchased 4,500 shares under its share repurchase program at an average share price of $30.13 (unadjusted for dividends paid) for an aggregate purchase price of $0.1 million.

    As of December 31, 2025, we had repurchased a total of 2,574,246 shares of common stock since emergence from bankruptcy at an average share price of $32.16 (unadjusted for dividends paid) for an aggregate purchase cost of $82.8 million.

  • The Company announced on March 6, 2026 that a quarterly cash dividend of $1.25 per share had been declared for the first quarter of 2026, to be paid on March 27, 2026 to shareholders of record as of the close of business on March 17, 2026.

data.title: Mattel bets on IP, gaming and entertainment to drive next phase of growth
data.description:
NEW YORK – Mattel (MAT) is accelerating its transformation from a traditional toy maker into a broader intellectual property and entertainment company, betting that its portfolio of global brands can generate growth across toys, film, gaming and live experiences.

Speaking at the UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference on Thursday, Mattel (MAT) Chairman and Chief Executive Ynon Kreiz said the company’s strategy centers on extracting more value from its intellectual property while expanding beyond physical toys.

“We introduced a new strategy to grow our IP-driven play and family entertainment business,” Kreiz said during the presentation. The plan focuses on managing both toys and entertainment holistically while capturing value from brand extensions beyond physical products.

Mattel (MAT) owns some of the most recognizable brands in children’s entertainment, including Barbie, Hot Wheels, Uno and American Girl. Kreiz said the company’s portfolio gives it a strong foundation as consumer brands become increasingly important across media and entertainment.

“These are vibrant, thriving cultural brands,” he said. “The journey we’ve been on is transforming Mattel (MAT) from a toy manufacturer into an IP-driven company.”

Strategy shifts toward IP-driven play and entertainment

Kreiz said Mattel (MAT)’s core toy business remains strong, supported by industry growth and continued consumer demand for play experiences.

The global toy industry has expanded in 23 of the past 25 years and has grown more than 3% annually over the last five years, he said. Parents continue to prioritize spending on children, and toys remain a strategic category for retailers because they drive store traffic and encourage additional spending.

Mattel (MAT) organizes its toy portfolio across six categories including dolls, vehicles, infant and preschool toys, action figures, building sets and games.

Innovation remains central to the company’s growth strategy, Kreiz said, along with opportunities in collectibles and direct-to-consumer products. The company’s Mattel Creations platform, which sells limited-edition products directly to fans, has been seeing double-digit growth.

Mattel (MAT) is also expanding partnerships with entertainment companies and intellectual property owners. Recent additions include licensing deals tied to DC characters such as Batman and Superman, as well as collaborations linked to Netflix and Paramount franchises.

Movies, games and partnerships set up 2026 inflection

Kreiz described 2026 as an “inflection year” for Mattel’s entertainment strategy.

Two major film releases are scheduled for next year: Masters of the Universe, which will debut in theaters, and Matchbox, which will launch on Apple TV+.

The films represent the next phase of Mattel’s push into entertainment following the success of the Barbie movie.

Kreiz said the company’s broader goal is to create a cycle in which entertainment success drives toy sales and vice versa.

“Success in toys drives success in entertainment, and success in entertainment drives even more success in toys,” he said.

Mattel (MAT) is also expanding into location-based entertainment. The company currently has seven permanent attractions in development including theme park concepts, water parks and other experiences tied to its brands.

Examples include Barbie cafés, Hot Wheels attractions and Uno-themed social clubs.

Gaming becomes a strategic priority

Digital gaming is emerging as a major growth opportunity.

Mattel (MAT) is expanding its self-publishing strategy in mobile gaming and recently moved to fully acquire Mattel163, a mobile gaming developer that previously operated as a joint venture.

The business, which generated more than $200 million in revenue, will now be fully integrated into Mattel’s gaming strategy.

The acquisition allows Mattel to coordinate game launches, cross-promote titles and better leverage its brand portfolio, Kreiz said.

“It’s about creative execution, user acquisition and monetization,” he said, describing the economics of self-published mobile games.

The company expects to have four global games in the market by 2027, including two launching in 2026 and at least two more in 2027.

Investments expected to pressure profit short term but boost growth in 2027

Mattel plans to invest roughly $150 million to expand gaming, digital marketing and other growth initiatives.

The spending will weigh on profitability in 2026 but is expected to generate returns beginning in 2027.

Much of the investment will support mobile gaming and performance marketing aimed at acquiring new users. Kreiz said the marketing spend is designed to deliver measurable returns because the company can track user acquisition and monetization directly.

Other investments include building-set expansion through the Mattel Brick Shop initiative and growing the trading card segment, which is one of the fastest-growing categories in toys.

Mattel (MAT) is also investing in new technologies including artificial intelligence and strengthening first-party data capabilities to improve direct engagement with consumers.

Brands, capital discipline underpin expansion plan

Mattel (MAT) expects revenue growth of 3% to 6% in 2026, driven by toy innovation, partnerships and early progress in gaming and entertainment.

Hot Wheels, Uno and Mattel Brick Shop are expected to remain key growth drivers.

Looking further ahead, Kreiz said 2027 should deliver faster revenue growth and double-digit operating income expansion as recent investments begin to pay off.

Entertainment revenue, which typically carries higher margins because it relies heavily on licensing and royalties, is expected to contribute meaningfully to profitability.

The company’s capital allocation priorities remain focused on organic investment, maintaining a strong balance sheet and selective acquisitions.

Mattel (MAT) has repurchased about $1.2 billion of shares over the past three years and plans to buy back an additional $1.5 billion over the next several years.

Kreiz said the company believes its shares remain undervalued relative to Mattel’s long-term potential.

“It's rare to have a company that owns such quality assets with built-in global fan bases,” he said. “The question now is not whether our brands can expand beyond toys, but how much further we can scale that opportunity.”
